abstract: Architected metamaterials offer the potential to achieve extraordinary mechanical properties by tailoring their spatial configurations. However, traditional inverse design methods struggle to replicate the full complexity of mechanical responses, such as non-linear stress-strain curves, due to the challenges of multi-objective optimization and inherent manufacturing defects. In this work, we present a rapid inverse design framework that utilizes generative machine learning and desktop additive manufacturing to create metamaterials based on user-prescribed uniaxial stress-strain curves. By integrating a surrogate forward neural network with a generative inverse model, our methodology effectively maps complex target behaviors to optimal topological parameters while explicitly accounting for process-dependent fabrication errors. We demonstrate that this approach can replicate a vast array of mechanical footprints, including monotonic and cyclic behaviors, with approximately 90% fidelity between target and experimental results. Furthermore, we illustrate the practical utility of this framework by designing an architected shoe midsole with spatially tailored propulsion and cushioning, as well as compound lattices with programmed, multi-step energy absorption. By bypassing conventional iterative design cycles, this methodology provides a robust pathway for the rapid realization of materials with fully customizable, non-linear mechanical functionalities.
